That $10,000 offer was a reference to the unfortunate bet Romney tried to make with Texas Governor Rick Perry during a recent debate.
During a visit to a clothing store, Romney did some Christmas shopping and spoke French with a woman from Quebec.
He's fluent from his years as a missionary in Paris.

In 2004, Republicans excoriated Presidential nominee John Kerry for speaking French on the campaign trail.
And the exchange could undermine this accusation that Romney leveled at President Obama all through the trip.
I think you're seeing a president who fundamentally believes we should be more like Europe.
This kind of retail politics is virtually required to win a New Hampshire primary.
Some voters here say they won't vote for any presidential candidate unless they've shaken hands with him.
So, Romney shook a lot of hands this week, because as he told a breakfast customer at a diner in Tilton, New Hampshire, I've tried winning and losing.
I prefer winning.
Ari Shapiro, NPR News, Concord, New Hampshire.

Harry Shearer is a man of many parts, best known for the voices he provides on ""The Simpsons,"" to characters including Montgomery Burns and Ned Flanders, and as an actor in films like ""Spinal Tap,"" a webcaster, a blogger for Huffington Post.
He uses his own voice as the host of ""Le Show"" on public radio stations.
But he also gets attention for presentations of significant silence.
Amid the noise of the 2008 election season, Shearer hosted online ""Silent Debate"" that paired up presidential candidates caught on camera in the moments before they went live.

An exhibit at the Aldrich Museum of Contemporary Art in Connecticut features videos that Shearer has collected of politicians and media personalities all taken in the moments before their images and voices are broadcast around the country.
If you would like to talk with Harry Shearer about his project, give us a call.
We'd also like to hear from those of you who make public appearances of one sort or another.
What's your routine for the minutes between the green room and the air?
The phone number is 800-989-8255.
E-mail is talk@npr.org.
You can also join the conversation online.
Go to npr.org, that's our website, and just click on Talk of the Nation.
Harry Shearer's video installation is called the ""Silent Echo Chambe,r"" and he joins us from the studio in Hollywood.
Happy New Year, Harry.
Thank you, Neal.
Same to you.
Oh, thank you.
Well, it's a little more complicated.
Somebody who works for the licensing company that I am under found me - a freelance worker.
She found me on MySpace and kind ushered me in the direction of the licensing company that then licensed my music to the show.
Sort of a chain of events that happened.
And everybody - it was just a fortuitous chain of events…
Pretty much, yeah.
She found me - and a friend of mine had put my - posted me on their MySpace, and she found me on that.
But we've learned since that there are companies that assign people just to go to places like your MySpace page and listen and see if maybe this is the next big, hot star.
Well, yeah.
And I think before MySpace and things of that nature came out, people - A&R people would go to shows - which they still do, I know.
But it's almost like you check out their MySpace page first to see what's up, and then you go check them out and see how they are live.
It's like a little preview, you know.
And it's such an easy way - if you hear a buzz about a musician, you just check them out on their MySpace page.
It's so easy, and it's so accessible to everybody.
And it's completely blown up in the past few years - two years, even.
I remember when I first signed up, I only did it because a couple of my friends were saying, oh, you know, I think it's a good idea for you to get a music page - a music MySpace page.
You know, everybody's doing it now.
And I was like, all right.
I'll do it.
And now, A&R people and music execs go to your MySpace page to see how many plays you have and how many friends and this and this, and obviously to listen to your music, but…
They look at those other things, too.
A&R, of course, originally stands for Artist and Repertory - various other people have come up with other things that it means, but we'll get into that here.
(Soundbite of laughter)
In the meantime, because you're unsigned, every time somebody downloads one of your tunes onto iTunes, you make a lot of money.
Yeah, I do get most of it.
You know, iTunes gets some, and then I get most of it.
Of the $.99, you get about two thirds of it.
Yup.
Just about, yeah.
And if you were signed, you would get much less than that.
Yes, I would.

The whole idea with signing is that they would get your music heard and promote you and then more people - many people would buy it, so percentage-wise, you'd end up with more than if you were selling it on your own, even getting most of the profits.
But with things like…
But from your experience, this math is turning around.
Well, with things like television, it's kind of creating this whole new era of independent musicians being able to survive without labels.
I'm not saying that I would never sign with a label.
That's definitely not where I'm at.
I don't really know.
I'm kind of enjoying where I am at right now, and seeing how long I can kind of stand on my own and how strong I can get.
But I'm definitely not opposed to the idea of more help, which is what I see a record label as of this point.
I don't see them as my savior.
I see them as…
Or as validation.
…a huge asset.
No, right - exactly.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Which I think is a really good place to be for somebody, you know.
It's business.
Don't think of it as a validation.
Exactly.
Yeah.
Exactly.
Our guest is Ingrid Michaelson, an unsigned musician who's - well, the talk of ""Grey's Anatomy"".
You're listening to TALK OF THE NATION from NPR News.
Let's get a caller on the line, and this is Josh.
Josh is with us from Lake Odessa in Michigan.
Hi.
Hi.
I just wanted to say that I'm also an independent musician, unsigned.
I have a MySpace music page, and it has done wonders for my band.
We have 3,600 plays here, and we just started up a couple months ago.
And it equals out to a couple hundred a month, which I'm really happy with at this point because I live in a nowhere town, and it's actually gotten us show offers.
It's gotten us offers to be on compilation discs for music in the area.
It's done a lot for us.

And it kind of evens the playing field with what record labels can offer, because you can say, you know, to them that we can do this by ourselves, and you're going to be a partner, as opposed the dominating force in my career…
Yeah.
…(unintelligible).
And I think what Ingrid was saying a few moments ago, it used to be that, indeed, all of the outlets were relatively controlled in terms of radio and shows and that sort of thing.
But TV - and again, Ingrid, we used to think of this as just, you know, what, ""Saturday Night Live"" or an appearance on a variety show or something like that.
Shows like ""Grey's Anatomy"" are using music in completely different ways.
Well, I think it's also - I just kind of figured this out the other day, because I used to watch a lot of shows like - I don't know if anybody remembers ""Felicity"".
It was a show that I was…
Short lived, yes.
